children.value: Water flows through a sluice gate as shown below. The friction losses through the gate are estimated to be 0.2 m. Find the horizontal thrust of the water per meter width on the sluice gate. Given: y

children.value:
from sympy.solvers import solve
from sympy import Symbol
v_1 = Symbol("v_1")
expr= y_1+v_1**2/(2*g)-y_2-(v_1*(y_1*b_1)/(y_2*b_2))**2/(2*g)
v_1 = solve((expr), (v_1))
v_1=round(v_1[1],2) #m/s

print('Solving for v_1 we have v_1=', v_1, "m/s")

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text: Solving for v_1 we have v_1= 2.03 m/s

%%render long 
v_2= v_1*(y_1*b_1)/(y_2*b_2) #m/s
Q= v_1*(y_1*b_1) #m3/s
A_1= b_1*y_1 #m2
A_2= b_2*y_2 #m2
R_x= rho_water*g*y_1/2*A_1-rho_water*g*y_2/2*A_2-rho_water*Q*(v_2-v_1) #N
R_x_= R_x/1000 #kN

The pressure at section 1 is 500 kPa, and the velocity at sections 1 is 6 m/s. Calculate the force of the water on the nozzle.

children.html: ∑Fx=ρQ(v2−v1)(NII)F1−Rx=ρQ(v2−v1)Rx=ρ1A1−ρQ(v2−v1)\begin{align*} \sum F_x = \rho Q (v_2 - v_1) \text (N \mathrm{II})\\ F_1 - R_x = \rho Q (v_2 - v_1)\\ R_x = \rho_{1} A_1 - \rho Q (v_2 - v_1)\\ \end{align*}∑Fx​=ρQ(v2​−v1​)(NII)F1​−Rx​=ρQ(v2​−v1​)Rx​=ρ1​A1​−ρQ(v2​−v1​)​

Problems for Lecture 30: Conservation of Linear Momentum 3

children.label: Problems for Lecture 30: Conservation of Linear Momentum 3

children.value: A 90° bend bend of a 300 mm diameter pipe lies in the horizontal plane. Water flows through the pipe  at a velocity of 3 m/s and the inlet of the bend has a pressure of 50 kPa.  Neglecting friction losses, calculate the resultant force of the water on the bend.
Use conservation of energy (Bernoulli equation) to find the exit pressure.
Apply conservation of momentum in the x and z directions to calculate the force components on the bend.

children.html: z1+p1ρg+v122g=z2+p2ρg+v222gz_1+\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=z_2+\frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\z1​+ρgp1​​+2gv12​​=z2​+ρgp2​​+2gv22​​

butz1=z2;bend in the horizontal planev1=v2;Conservation of massthusp1=p2\begin{align*} \text{but} \quad z_1 &= z_2 \quad;\quad \text{bend in the horizontal plane}\\ v_1 &= v_2 \quad; \quad \text{Conservation of mass}\\ \text{thus} \quad p_1 &= p_2 \end{align*}butz1​v1​thusp1​​=z2​;bend in the horizontal plane=v2​;Conservation of mass=p2​​
∑Fx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)\sum F_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\∑Fx​=ρQ(v2x​−v1x​)
Rx−p2A2=ρQ(v2x−v1x)R_x-p_2A_2 = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\Rx​−p2​A2​=ρQ(v2x​−v1x​)
∑Fz=ρQ(v2z−v1z)\sum F_z = \rho Q (v_{2z}-v_{1z})\\∑Fz​=ρQ(v2z​−v1z​)
p1A1−Rz=ρQ(v2z−v1z)p_1A_1-R_z = \rho Q (v_{2z}-v_{1z})\\p1​A1​−Rz​=ρQ(v2z​−v1z​)

children.value:
%%render long
v_1= 3 #m/s
p_1=50*1000 #Pa
D=300/1000 #m
v_2 = v_1 #m/s|
A= (pi*D**2)/4 #m2
Q= v_1*A #m3/s
p_2= p_1 #Pa
R_x= p_2*A+rho_water*Q*(v_2-0) #N
R_z= p_1*A-rho_water*Q*(0-v_1) #N
R= sqrt(R_x**2+R_z**2)#N
The force of the water on the bend is the same size, but opposite in direction.

A reducing right-angled bend lies in the horizontal plane. Water enters from the west at a velocity of 3 m/s and a pressure of 50 kPa, and leaves toward the north. The diameter of the entrance is 500 mm and at the exit 250 mm. Neglecting friction losses, find the magnitude of the x and y components of the resultant force on the bend.

children.html: 11.6  kN  ;  6.2  kN 11.6 \; kN\;;\;6.2\;kN11.6kN;6.2kN

children.value: Use conservation of energy (Bernoulli equation) to find the exit pressure.
Apply conservation of momentum in the x and z directions to calculate the force components on the bend.

children.html: z1+p1ρg+v122g=z2+p2ρg+v222gbutz1=z2;horizontal planep1ρg+v122g=p2ρg+v222gp2=(p1ρg+v122g−v222g)ρg\begin{align*} z_1+\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g} &= z_2+\frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\ \text{but} \quad z_1 &= z_2 \quad ; \quad \text{horizontal plane}\\ \frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g} &= \frac {p_2}{\rho g}+\frac{v_2^2}{2g}\\ p_2 &= (\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}-\frac{v_2^2}{2g}) \rho g \end{align*}z1​+ρgp1​​+2gv12​​butz1​ρgp1​​+2gv12​​p2​​=z2​+ρgp2​​+2gv22​​=z2​;horizontal plane=ρgp2​​+2gv22​​=(ρgp1​​+2gv12​​−2gv22​​)ρg​
∑Fx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)p1A1−Rx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)\sum F_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\ p_1A_1-R_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\∑Fx​=ρQ(v2x​−v1x​)p1​A1​−Rx​=ρQ(v2x​−v1x​)
∑Fz=ρQ(v2z−v1z)Rz−p2A2=ρQ(v2z−v1z)\sum F_z = \rho Q (v_{2z}-v_{1z})\\ R_z-p_2A_2 = \rho Q (v_{2z}-v_{1z})\\∑Fz​=ρQ(v2z​−v1z​)Rz​−p2​A2​=ρQ(v2z​−v1z​)

A perforated plate with 50% of its area consisting of small holes is installed at the outlet of a 30 cm diameter horizontal water pipe carrying water at a velocity of 2 m/s. Neglect energy losses and calculate the force of the water on the perforated plate. Assume that the pressure head in the pipe is much greater than it’s diameter, so that you can use a uniform pressure distribution for any pipe cross section.

children.html: 142  N  to  the  right 142 \; N\;to\;the\;right142Ntotheright

children.value: Use conservation of mass to find the water velocity through the holes.
Apply the Bernoulli euqation to find the pressure difference.
Use conservation of momentum to calculate the force on the perforated plate.

wherez1=z2;horizontal planep2=0;free jet\begin{align*} \text{where} \quad z_1 &= z_2 \quad ; \quad \text{horizontal plane}\\ p_2 &= 0 \quad ; \quad \text{free jet} \end{align*}wherez1​p2​​=z2​;horizontal plane=0;free jet​
p1ρg=v222g−v122g\frac {p_1}{\rho g}=\frac{v_2^2}{2g}-\frac{v_1^2}{2g}ρgp1​​=2gv22​​−2gv12​​
∑Fx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)p1A1−Rx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)\sum F_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\ p_1 A_1 - R_x = \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\∑Fx​=ρQ(v2x​−v1x​)p1​A1​−Rx​=ρQ(v2x​−v1x​)

children.value: The force on the plate is 141.4 N to the right.(NⅡ)

The figure below shows a pipe that ends in a set of two nozzles (points 2 and 3) at right angles to each other, and exiting into the atmosphere. The nozzles are in the horizontal plane. The pipe has a diameter of 150 mm and nozzles 2 and 3 have exit diameters of 100 and 50 mm respectively. The exit velocity at both points 2 and 3 is 10 m/s. Assume ideal flow.Calculate the following:
a)	The flow rate and velocity at at point 1.
c)	The size and direction of the force that the fluid exerts on the pipe.

d)	Assume the pressure at point 1 remains as in (b), but an energy headloss of 0.5 m occurs through each of the nozzles at points 2 and 3. Calculate the flow rate in the system and the velocities at points 1 and 2. Ignore energy losses other that those at the nozzles and assume the distribution of the flow between 2 and 3 remains the same as in (a).

children.html: a)  5.55  m/s a) \;5.55 \; m/sa)5.55m/s
b)  34.57  kPa b) \;34.57 \; kPab)34.57kPa
c)  420  N  at  27.9  °  above  East c) \;420 \; N\; at\; 27.9\; °\; above\; Eastc)420Nat27.9°aboveEast
d)  91  L/s  ;  5.15  m/s  ;  9.3  m/s d) \;91 \;L/s\;;\; 5.15\; m/s\;;\; 9.3 \;m/sd)91L/s;5.15m/s;9.3m/s

children.value: To calculate the flow rate and velocity in point 1 the conservation of mass can be used:

children.html: p1ρg=v222g−v122g\frac {p_1}{\rho g}=\frac{v_2^2}{2g}-\frac{v_1^2}{2g}\\ρgp1​​=2gv22​​−2gv12​​

text/latex.content:
\[
\begin{aligned}
p_{1} &= \left( \frac{ \left( v_{2} \right) ^{ 2 } - \left( v_{1} \right) ^{ 2 } }{ 2 \cdot g } \right) \cdot \rho_{water} \cdot g \\&= \left( \frac{ \left( 10 \right) ^{ 2 } - \left( 5.56 \right) ^{ 2 } }{ 2 \cdot 9.81 } \right) \cdot 1000 \cdot 9.81 \\&= 34567.90 \; \;\textrm{(Pa)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
p_{1_{}} &= \frac{ p_{1} }{ 1000 } \\&= \frac{ 34567.90 }{ 1000 } \\&= 34.57 \; \;\textrm{(kPa)}\\[10pt]
\end{aligned}
\]

children.value:
%%render long 2
F_x= p_1*A_1-rho_water*Q_2*v_2+rho_water*Q_1*v_1 #N
F_y= rho_water*Q_3*-v_3#N
F= sqrt(F_x**2+F_y**2) #N
theta= degrees(atan(abs(F_y)/F_x))

text/latex.content:
\[
\begin{aligned}
F_{x} &= p_{1} \cdot A_{1} - \rho_{water} \cdot Q_{2} \cdot v_{2} + \rho_{water} \cdot Q_{1} \cdot v_{1} \\&= 34567.90 \cdot 0.02 - 1000 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 10 + 1000 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 5.56 \\&= 370.88 \; \;\textrm{(N)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
F_{y} &= \rho_{water} \cdot Q_{3} \cdot \left( - v_{3} \right) \\&= 1000 \cdot 0.02 \cdot \left( - 10 \right) \\&= -196.35 \; \;\textrm{(N)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
F &= \sqrt { \left( F_{x} \right) ^{ 2 } + \left( F_{y} \right) ^{ 2 } } \\&= \sqrt { \left( 370.88 \right) ^{ 2 } + \left( -196.35 \right) ^{ 2 } } \\&= 419.65 \; \;\textrm{(N)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
\theta &= \operatorname{degrees} { \left( \arctan \left( \frac{ \operatorname{abs} { \left( F_{y} \right) } }{ F_{x} } \right) \right) } \\&= \operatorname{degrees} { \left( \arctan \left( \frac{ \operatorname{abs} { \left( -196.35 \right) } }{ 370.88 } \right) \right) } \\&= 27.90  \\[10pt]
\end{aligned}
\]

z1=z2z_1=z_2z1​=z2​
p2=0p_2=0p2​=0

children.html: Q2=0.07890.0981Q_2= \frac {0.0789}{0.0981}\\Q2​=0.09810.0789​
Q2=0.80Q1Q_2=0.80 Q_1\\Q2​=0.80Q1​
Q3=0.2Q1Q_3=0.2 Q_1\\Q3​=0.2Q1​
v2A2=0.80v1A1v_2A_2=0.80v_1A_1\\v2​A2​=0.80v1​A1​
v2πD224=0.80v1πD124v_2 \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4}=0.80 v_1\frac{\pi D_1^2}{4}\\v2​4πD22​​=0.80v1​4πD12​​
v2D22=0.80v1D12v_2 D_2^2=0.80 v_1 D_1^2\\v2​D22​=0.80v1​D12​
v2(0.1)2=0.80v1(0.15)2v_2 (0.1)^2=0.80 v_1 (0.15)^2\\v2​(0.1)2=0.80v1​(0.15)2
v2=1.8v1v_2= 1.8 v_1v2​=1.8v1​

children.value: Using conservation of energy and mass with an energy headloss of 0.5 m, we have:

children.html: p1ρg+v122g=(1.8v1)22g+hf\frac {p_1}{\rho g}+\frac{v_1^2}{2g}=\frac{(1.8 v_1)^2}{2g}+h_f\\ρgp1​​+2gv12​​=2g(1.8v1​)2​+hf​

children.value:
%%render long 2
h_f=0.5 #m
ratio_Q= Q_2/Q_1
ratio_v= ratio_Q*D_1**2/D_2**2
v_1= sqrt(((p_1/(rho_water*g)-h_f)*2*g)/(ratio_v**2-1))#m/s
v_2= ratio_v*v_1#m/s
Q= (v_1*A_1)*1000 #L/s

text/latex.content:
\[
\begin{aligned}
h_{f} &= 0.50 \; \;\textrm{(m)}
\\[10pt]
\mathrm{ratio}_{Q} &= \frac{ Q_{2} }{ Q_{1} } \\&= \frac{ 0.08 }{ 0.10 } \\&= 0.80  \\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
\mathrm{ratio}_{v} &= \mathrm{ratio}_{Q} \cdot \frac{ \left( D_{1} \right) ^{ 2 } }{ \left( D_{2} \right) ^{ 2 } } \\&= 0.80 \cdot \frac{ \left( 0.15 \right) ^{ 2 } }{ \left( 0.10 \right) ^{ 2 } } \\&= 1.80  \\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
v_{1} &= \sqrt { \frac{ \left( \frac{ p_{1} }{ \rho_{water} \cdot g } - h_{f} \right) \cdot 2 \cdot g }{ \left( \mathrm{ratio}_{v} \right) ^{ 2 } - 1 } } \\&= \sqrt { \frac{ \left( \frac{ 34567.90 }{ 1000 \cdot 9.81 } - 0.50 \right) \cdot 2 \cdot 9.81 }{ \left( 1.80 \right) ^{ 2 } - 1 } } \\&= 5.15 \; \;\textrm{(m/s)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
v_{2} &= \mathrm{ratio}_{v} \cdot v_{1} \\&= 1.80 \cdot 5.15 \\&= 9.26 \; \;\textrm{(m/s)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
Q &= \left( v_{1} \cdot A_{1} \right) \cdot 1000 \\&= \left( 5.15 \cdot 0.02 \right) \cdot 1000 \\&= 90.94 \; \;\textrm{(L/s)}\\[10pt]
\end{aligned}
\]

children.value: Problems for Lecture 31: Conservation of Linear Momentum 4

children.label: Problems for Lecture 31: Conservation of Linear Momentum 4

children.value: A 40 mm diameter jet has a velocity of 25 m/s. If this jet were to strike a large flat plat normally, what would the resultant force on the plate be?

children.html: 785  N 785 \; N785N

children.value: The jet direction will be parallel to the plate after hitting it.
Assume the jet speed remains constant.

children.html: ∑Fx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)−R=ρQ(0−v1)\sum F_x = \rho Q (v_{2x} - v_{1x})\\ -R = \rho Q (0 - v_1)\\∑Fx​=ρQ(v2x​−v1x​)−R=ρQ(0−v1​)

children.value:
%%render long
D_1= 40/1000 #m
v_1= 25 #m/s

A_1= pi*D_1**2/4 #m2
R_x= rho_water*A_1*v_1*(v_1-0) #N

Water hits the curved plate shown below that entirely reverses the flow direction. The diameter of the jet is 50 mm and it hits the plate at a velocity of 12 m/s. The water leaves the plate at the same velocity, but in the opposite direction. Find the force exerted by the water on the vane.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/141.png")
img

children.html: 565  N  to  the  right 565 \; N\; to\; the\; right565Ntotheright

children.value: The jet direction will be parallel to the plate after hitting it.
Assume the jet speed remains constant.

children.html: ∑Fx=ρQ(voutx−vinx)−Rx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)\sum F_x= \rho Q(v_{out_x}-v_{in_x})\\ -R_x = \rho Q(v_{2x} - v_{1x})∑Fx​=ρQ(voutx​​−vinx​​)−Rx​=ρQ(v2x​−v1x​)

children.value:
%%render long 3
D= 50/1000 #m 
v_1= 12 #m/s
v_2= -12 #m/s

A= pi*D**2/4 #m2
Q= v_1*A #m3/s
R_x= -rho_water*Q*(v_2-v_1) #N
The force of the water on vane is 565 N to the right.(NⅢ)

A jet is bent through an angle θ = 120° by a blade in the horizontal plane as shown below. The water jet has a velocity of 25 m/s and a diameter of 20 mm. Calculate the size and direction of the force on the blade.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/142.png")
img

children.html: 340  N  at  30  °  above  the  horizon 340 \; N\;at\;30\;°\;above\;the\;horizon340Nat30°abovethehorizon

children.value: The jet direction will be parallel to the plate after hitting it.
Assume the jet speed remains constant.

children.html: ∑Fx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)−Rx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)\sum F_x= \rho Q(v_{2_x}-v_{1_x})\\ -R_x= \rho Q(v_{2_x}-v_{1_x})\\∑Fx​=ρQ(v2x​​−v1x​​)−Rx​=ρQ(v2x​​−v1x​​)

children.value:
\begin{align*}
v_1 & = 25 \quad \text{m/s}\\
v_2 &= v_1\\
v_{1x} &= v_1\\
v_{1y} &= 0\\
v_{2x} &= -v_2 \sin \theta\\
v_{2y} &= -v_2 \cos \theta\\
-R_x &= \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\
-R_y &= \rho Q (v_{2y}-v_{1y})\\
\end{align*}
children.html: v1=25m/sv2=v1v1x=v1v1y=0v2x=−v2sin⁡θv2y=−v2cos⁡θ−Rx=ρQ(v2x−v1x)−Ry=ρQ(v2y−v1y)\begin{align*} v_1 & = 25 \quad \text{m/s}\\ v_2 &= v_1\\ v_{1x} &= v_1\\ v_{1y} &= 0\\ v_{2x} &= -v_2 \sin \theta\\ v_{2y} &= -v_2 \cos \theta\\ -R_x &= \rho Q (v_{2x}-v_{1x})\\ -R_y &= \rho Q (v_{2y}-v_{1y})\\ \end{align*}v1​v2​v1x​v1y​v2x​v2y​−Rx​−Ry​​=25m/s=v1​=v1​=0=−v2​sinθ=−v2​cosθ=ρQ(v2x​−v1x​)=ρQ(v2y​−v1y​)​

children.value:
%%render long 4
theta= 120 #°
v=25 #m/s
D=20/1000 #m

A= pi*D**2/4 #m2
Q= v*A #m3/s

alpha= 180-theta #°

R_x= -rho_water*Q*(-v*cos(radians(alpha))-v)#N
R_y= -rho_water*Q*(-v*sin(radians(alpha))-0)#N

F= sqrt(R_x**2+R_y**2) #N

theta_F= degrees(atan(R_y/R_x)) #°

text/latex.content:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 120 \; \;\textrm{(°)}
\\[10pt]
v &= 25 \; \;\textrm{(m/s)}
\\[10pt]
D &= 0.0200 \; \;\textrm{(m)}
\\[10pt]
A &= \pi \cdot \frac{ \left( D \right) ^{ 2 } }{ 4 } \\&= 3.1416 \cdot \frac{ \left( 0.0200 \right) ^{ 2 } }{ 4 } \\&= 0.0003 \; \;\textrm{(m2)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
Q &= v \cdot A \\&= 25 \cdot 0.0003 \\&= 0.0079 \; \;\textrm{(m3/s)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
\alpha &= 180 - \theta \\&= 180 - 120 \\&= 60 \; \;\textrm{(°)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
R_{x} &= \left( - \rho_{water} \right) \cdot Q \cdot \left( \left( - v \right) \cdot \cos { \left( \operatorname{radians} \alpha \right) } - v \right) \\&= \left( - 1000 \right) \cdot 0.0079 \cdot \left( \left( - 25 \right) \cdot \cos { \left( \operatorname{radians} 60 \right) } - 25 \right) \\&= 294.5243 \; \;\textrm{(N)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
R_{y} &= \left( - \rho_{water} \right) \cdot Q \cdot \left( \left( - v \right) \cdot \sin { \left( \operatorname{radians} \alpha \right) } - 0 \right) \\&= \left( - 1000 \right) \cdot 0.0079 \cdot \left( \left( - 25 \right) \cdot \sin { \left( \operatorname{radians} 60 \right) } - 0 \right) \\&= 170.0437 \; \;\textrm{(N)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
F &= \sqrt { \left( R_{x} \right) ^{ 2 } + \left( R_{y} \right) ^{ 2 } } \\&= \sqrt { \left( 294.5243 \right) ^{ 2 } + \left( 170.0437 \right) ^{ 2 } } \\&= 340.0874 \; \;\textrm{(N)}\\[10pt]
\\[10pt]
\theta_{F} &= \operatorname{degrees} { \left( \arctan \left( \frac{ R_{y} }{ R_{x} } \right) \right) } \\&= \operatorname{degrees} { \left( \arctan \left( \frac{ 170.0437 }{ 294.5243 } \right) \right) } \\&= 30.0000 \; \;\textrm{(°)}\\[10pt]
\end{aligned}
\]

children.value: A jet of water with a velocity of 25 m/s and diameter of 40 mm strikes a blade in a horizontal plane. If the angle of deflection is 115° and friction reduces the velocity to 22 m/s, find;
a.	the component of the force acting on the blade in the direction of the jet.
b.	the component of the force normal/ perpendicular to the jet.
c.	the magnitude and direction of the resultant force exerted on the blade.

from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(r"figures/143.png")
img

b)  0.626  kN  downwards b) \;0.626 \; kN\; downwardsb)0.626kNdownwards
c)  1.246  kN  at  30.2  °  below  east c) \;1.246 \; kN\; at\;30.2\;°\;below\;eastc)1.246kNat30.2°beloweast

children.value: Problems	for	Lecture	32: Transients	– Elastic	fluid	in	a	rigid	pipe

children.label: Problems	for	Lecture	32: Transients	– Elastic	fluid	in	a	rigid	pipe

children.value: A 2 km long, 500 mm diameter pipeline carries water at a velocity of 2 m/s.  Calculate the pressure rise in the system if water assuming a rigid pipe and a water temperature of 15 °Celsius.

children.html: 298  m 298 \; m298m

children.value: Calculate the celerity of the transient wave c.
Calculate the pressure increase using the Joukowski equation.
To calculate the pressure rise in the system the water hammer equation can be used.

children.html: c=EvρwaterΔp=ρcvoht=Δpρwatergc= \sqrt {E_v}{\rho_{water}}\\ \Delta p= \rho c v_o\\ h_t= \frac {\Delta p}{\rho_{water} g}c=Ev​​ρwater​Δp=ρcvo​ht​=ρwater​gΔp​

children.value: The bulk modulus of elasticity can be found in Table A.1 for 15°C

children.html: Ev=2.14x106kN/m2E_v= 2.14 x10^6 kN/m^2Ev​=2.14x106kN/m2

children.value:
%%render long 2
D= 500#mm 
L= 2 #km 
v= 2 #m/s

E_v=2.14*10**9 #N/m2
c= sqrt (E_v/(rho_water)) #m/s
Delta_P= rho_water*c*v #Pa 
h_t= Delta_P/ (rho_water*g) #m

Calculate the celerity of a pressure wave in water at 20 °C assuming a water density of 1000 kg/m3 . What difference does it make to your answer if change in water density with temperature is taken into account?

children.html: 1476.48  m/s  1477.81  m/s  ,  thus  less  than  0.1  %  difference 1476.48 \; m/s\; 1477.81\; m/s\; ,\; thus\; less\; than\; 0.1\; \% \; difference1476.48m/s1477.81m/s,thuslessthan0.1%difference

children.value: Calculate the celerity of the transient wave c.
Calculate the pressure increase using the Joukowski equation.

children.html: c=Evρc= \sqrt {\frac{E_v}{\rho}}c=ρEv​​​

children.value: From Table A.1, the density of water at 20 °C is

children.html: 998.2kg/m3998.2 kg/m^3998.2kg/m3
Ev=1030x106N/m2E_v= 1030 x10^6 N/m^2Ev​=1030x106N/m2

children.value: The effect of the density can be calculated with the difference between the celerities:

children.html: difference=(c2−c1c2)100\text{difference} = \left( \frac{c_2 - c_1}{c_2} \right) 100difference=(c2​c2​−c1​​)100

children.value:
%%render long 2
T=20 #°C
rho=1000 #kg/m3

rho_20=998.2 #kg/m3
E_v=2.18*10**6*1000 #N/m2
c_1= sqrt(E_v/rho) #m/s
c_2= sqrt(E_v/rho_20) #m/s
difference= ((c_2-c_1)/c_2)*100 #%

Find the celerity of a pressure wave in benzene if a rigid pipe is assumed. If the initial flow velocity is 2 m/s, what will the increased pressure and pressure head be if a valve in the pipe is suddenly closed?

children.html: 1081.88  m/s  ;  1.9  MPa  ;  220.57  m  benzene \;1081.88\; m/s\;;\; 1.9\; MPa\;; \;220.57\; m \;benzene1081.88m/s;1.9MPa;220.57mbenzene

children.value: Calculate the celerity of the transient wave c.
Calculate the pressure increase using the Joukowski equation.
To calculate the pressure rise in the system, the water hammer equation can be used:

children.html: c=EvρwaterΔp=ρcv\begin{align*} c &= \sqrt{ \frac{E_v}{\rho_{\text{water}}} } \\ \Delta p &= \rho c v \end{align*}cΔp​=ρwater​Ev​​​=ρcv​

children.value: After calculating the pressure increase, the next step is to determine the pressure head:

children.html: H=Δpρwaterg\begin{align*} H &= \frac{ \Delta p }{ \rho_{\text{water}} g } \end{align*}H​=ρwater​gΔp​​
S=0.88S = 0.88S=0.88
Ev=1030x106N/m2E_v= 1030 x10^6 N/m^2Ev​=1030x106N/m2

children.value:
%%render long 2
V = 2#m/s

S=0.88 
rho_benz= S*rho_water #kg/m3
E_v=1030*10**6 #N/m2
c= sqrt (E_v/(S*rho_water)) #m/s
Delta_P= rho_benz*c*V #Pa 
Delta_P_Mpa= Delta_P/1000000 #MPa
H= Delta_P/ (rho_benz*g) #m
